speaker: interviewer
text: Sure.

speaker: interviewer
text: OK.

speaker: interviewer
text: OK. And then-- so the song goes, what? G-L-O-R-I-A. Gloria!

speaker: interviewer
text: Which one are you doing? [INAUDIBLE]

speaker: interviewer
text: OK. We're rolling.

speaker: interviewer
text: This is an interview with Gloria Ungar. Our date is January 18, 1995. We're in Los Angeles. The interviewer is Sandy Jacobson. Gloria, thank you so much for participating in the project. Tell me, please, your name, your maiden name-- the name you were born with-- and when, when you were born.

speaker: interviewee
text: My name is Gloria Ungar. And I was born September 9, 1930 in Krosno, Poland. At that time, my name was Gitta Nagel. When I came to the United States and I started school, my friends decided that I have to change my name to Gloria. So I remained Gloria.

speaker: interviewer
text: Tell me about your family, please.

speaker: interviewee
text: Well, [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] my mother originally came from Slovakia. And she married my father, who lived in Poland. She moved to Krosno, Poland, which was a town in Galicia. My father was an educated man. He had [NON-ENGLISH] from the Krosno rabbi, which is-- he, he was a rabbi, actually, but he did not practice being a rabbi. He, he was a businessman, businessman. He was a wealthy man. We had a lot of real estate in Poland. And we had many properties. He was a wealthy man.

speaker: interviewer
text: Did you have brothers or sisters?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes. I had three brothers, but only one survived. His name is Jack Nagel. And he lives right here in Los Angeles. My other brothers did not survive. [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] The war broke out, I believe, September the 1st, just before my, my ninth birthday. And I remember right after the war broke out-- it must have been the next day-- they started bombing our city. We had a small airport, and that was bombed right away. And right after that, the Germans came in, the armored vehicles and, and the army. And they were just marching and marching and marching. And it remains in my mind because I was a little girl, and I was so scared. I was standing behind the window and just watching them come and come and come. And then things sort of settled down. And the first thing they did is they started on the Jews, you know. They confiscated all the properties. They wanted-- we knew that my father was in jeopardy because he was a wealthy man. And that was the first thing they wanted, to take all the wealthy people and their properties.

speaker: interviewer
text: So we managed to smuggle my father out to Czechoslovakia, where we had my mother's family. You know, for money, you could still do some things, you know. So he was the first one to, to leave for Czechoslovakia.

speaker: interviewee
text: My brother, my surviving brother, who was at that time perhaps I would say maybe 16 years old, was the next in line to go to Czechoslovakia, because they would have taken him to labor camp. [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] So he was next on the list. And he was also smuggled to Slovakia. We couldn't all go together because it was too dangerous. They wouldn't take more than one, two people at a time through the forest. After a while, things were-- became very unbearable. [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] My other brother-- his name was [NON-ENGLISH]. His Hebrew name was Mordechai. He was four years older than I. He was the one who was taken to work. And he had to supply food for us. He was the bad boy in the family. But the minute the trouble started, he was, he was our father. And he just took care of everything. He went to work. And he would steal food and bring it home. And unfortunately, my mother became very sick. She found out that she has cancer. And there, there was no medical care for her. So the next thing was to get my mother over to Slovakia, where she could get some medical help. Well, after a couple of months-- I don't remember when that was. That must have been already 1940. We got her through to Slovakia. [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] The next people to go was myself and my younger brother, Nathan, who was a year and a half younger than me, and a cousin. Her name was Gitcha Nagel. She was 18 years old. And the three of us were supposed to be smuggled to Slovakia. Well, when we came to the border, where we were supposed to go through the forest, a guide, the Polish guide-- they were paid for in advance, half in advance and half after we would be delivered to Slovakia. Well, when we, when we got into the forest during the night, it was very, very scary. I mean, it was dark. And the searchlights were going all over. And the dogs were barking. And it was just terribly terrifying. The branches were scratching us. And we were bloody. And we didn't know where to go. And my oldest cousin, she refused to go on. She was so terrified that she said, just leave me here. And just let me die here. I just don't want to go on anymore. My little brother couldn't walk anymore. He was just-- he just couldn't walk anymore. He just refused to walk. And I, all of-- I think maybe I must have been 11 or 12-- I just had to drag them and beg them, just we will be soon there. Please hold on. We'll be soon there. The guide refused to go on. He said, I'm not going any further because they're going to get us. You hear the dogs, and you see the searchlights. And if they catch us, not only you will be killed-- shot-- but I will be shot, too. Well, I just cried and begged and held onto his hand. And I told him, look, I have a rich father. He'll make you so wealthy. You'll see. It will be worth your while. Please don't leave us here. We managed to get to Slovakia. When we came to Slovakia, there was a wagon waiting for us with hay. And they put us into the wagon, and they covered us with hay so that no one should see us until we get to, to Slovakia to the little town of Bardejov.

speaker: interviewer
text: And when we came there, we were brought into a-- [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] what would you call it? A little, a little place where the neighbors couldn't see when we get out of the hay. And there was my father, mother, and older brother waiting for us. And you can imagine that the joy was very big.

speaker: interviewee
text: And the next, the next person to come-- was supposed to come-- my brother, my, Nathan, my, Nathan with my grandmother, who lived with us. But unfortunately, they never made it, because the Germans came in one day into the house. And my, my grandmother was in bed. And she was an elderly lady. And they said she should get up because they are taking her away. And she just refused to get up. And they shot her right there in her bed. And they took my brother away. And we never heard of him. We never heard of him. He never survived. And so in Slovakia, it was relatively free. We had relatives, and they took us in. And they gave us accommodations. And I even went to school. Of course, they didn't let us go to public schools, but we had a Jewish school. And they had one class which accommodated all the grades, you know, from one to eight. And we had to wear the star. And we were afraid. You know, kids walk on the street. But if you saw a Gentile kid or-- you just stepped off the sidewalk because you were afraid. But we managed to have fun anyway, you know. We were kids. We managed to have fun anyway. And--

speaker: interviewer
text: Tell me how you-- what kind of fun you did, you had. Do you remember what kind of games you would play?

speaker: interviewee
text: What kind, kind of games we would have? Well, it wasn't really-- I mean, we, we were always persecuted. We were always worried, you know. I mean, even in Slovakia, we were all the time in hiding.

speaker: interviewer
text: It wasn't like they would come every day. We would know in advance-- sometimes we would know in advance when the razia would be, because they paid the Slovaks. Some of them, you could pay them money, and they would warn you. I don't know what you call razia. You ever heard that word, razia? Is that like an Aktion, where they would come?

speaker: interviewee
text: Right. They would come and get them. So we would go into hiding, you know. We had a few hiding places.

speaker: interviewer
text: Describe your hiding places.

speaker: interviewee
text: There were attics by Gentile families who were paid to keep us there. Or there would be cellars. Or we would go into the forest. And until we heard that everything's quiet, that the transport is gone, whomever they collected, they took, and for the time being, it was quiet.

speaker: interviewer
text: I'd like to talk a little bit about your life, both in Slovakia and also in your life in, in Poland before the war started. Let's go-- let's flash back to, to Poland. You came from a wealthy family. Do you remember the house you used to live in?

speaker: interviewee
text: Oh, yes.

speaker: interviewer
text: Can you describe it?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes, I remember the house. As a matter of fact, two years ago, I went back with my husband and my brother and his five-- and the children-- back to Poland. It was very traumatic for me, but everything came back, you know. The house where we lived-- I looked around. And I just-- I was looking up the window, and I said, oh, my god, my mother used to call us from that window. Children, come on up. It's time to eat lunch or eat, eat dinner. We had a-- our house was-- I had many cousins living all around us. We were-- lived in a house with one, with two aunts and uncles and their children. On the other side, there was a house. It was also our house. We had many relatives and children. And we were all very happy. There were lots of children playing, happy. Even I remember when the Germans came in, the grownups would say, children, you go and play. You know, they didn't want us to worry. So we would go outside and play. But we were very worried. We knew. Children feel it. And they know it. And we knew that, you know, something bad is happening. We knew that things are happening [INAUDIBLE]. But before the war, I don't remember too much. I just remember my family, going to school, and being just a very happy family.

speaker: interviewer
text: Do you remember your synagogue?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes, I remember the synagogue. I remember my father taking me on, on the Sabbath to the synagogue. And-- but mostly I remember my family-- the cousins, which none of them survived from Poland. And in Slovakia, we had my mother's family, so--

speaker: interviewer
text: Did you know that family before you came to Slovakia?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes. Yes, we kept in touch. And my mother's sister, she lived with us before the war. So that when the war broke out-- when we went back to Slovakia, we went back to her. And she was wonderful to us. Her family was wonderful to us.

speaker: interviewer
text: Was it your mother's younger sister, older sister?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes, my mother's younger sister.

speaker: interviewer
text: Did she have a family of her own?

speaker: interviewee
text: She had a-- yes, she had a family of her own. As a matter of fact, I'll tell you about her because I met her in Bergen-Belsen and what she did for me.

speaker: interviewer
text: Please, tell me. Oh, when we get to Bergen-Belsen.

speaker: interviewee
text: Yeah.

speaker: interviewer
text: All right. You'll tell us then.

speaker: interviewee
text: Yeah.

speaker: interviewer
text: Tell me about-- was your family religious? Were they traditional?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes. Yes. Yes, absolutely. We were-- most Jews were religious. Even the ones that were not religious, they were, they were-- well, what would you call it? They were, they were still religious. Maybe not Orthodox, but religious. Or at least the ones that I knew-- everybody was religious.

speaker: interviewer
text: Right. So you observed all of the holidays and the Shabbat?

speaker: interviewee
text: We observed all the holidays. I went to public school, but I also went to a Jewish school, where I learned Hebrew-- how to read and write and the--

speaker: interviewer
text: When you went to the synagogue with your father, did you sit upstairs or on another side?

speaker: interviewee
text: No, I sat with my father. I was a little girl. Children are allowed to stay with their parents.

speaker: interviewer
text: Mm-hm. Your mother didn't go to the temple?

speaker: interviewee
text: No. Women didn't go to temple on Saturdays. They were home, prepared, made lunch, you know.

speaker: interviewer
text: Do you remember those Sabbath lunches?

speaker: interviewee
text: Oh, sure, I remember. I remember the holidays. I remember Simchat Torah, which was a holiday. And they just prepared stuffed cabbage and cakes and everything. And the whole town came for the meal. I mean, my, my father was a wealthy man. He opened his door to everybody. It was just wonderful. It was a wonderful life. I mean, it's hard to believe that all is gone.

speaker: interviewer
text: When you talk about the father opening the door, was it-- were people-- were they friends or the poor in the town?

speaker: interviewee
text: No, they were friends and also poor people, people that didn't have anywhere to go. They-- every Sabbath my father would bring people along for lunch, people that didn't, you know, that didn't have anywhere to go.

speaker: interviewer
text: Did your mother do the cooking, or did somebody else help her?

speaker: interviewee
text: Well, she had help, of course. My mother was a business lady, but we had our grandmother living with us, and we had help.

speaker: interviewer
text: What did your mother do?

speaker: interviewee
text: She helped out in the business.

speaker: interviewer
text: Mm-hm. What kind of a business? Can you describe it?

speaker: interviewee
text: Well, my father had real estate. We had four big, [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] big houses. At that time, it was considered big. You know, today maybe it's not big. But as I went back two years ago to Poland, I still think it was a very nice building. We had a, a new apartment building, which was, had, I think, eight units, which was considered a nice, modern building.

speaker: interviewer
text: And we had other businesses. My father had, [PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] he had some oil, oil royalties coming to him. And he had-- how do you call it in English? [NON-ENGLISH], which is called-- they, they were selling wood for builders and things like that. Like a lumber yard, kind of?

speaker: interviewee
text: Like a lumber yard, yes.

speaker: interviewer
text: Now, do you remember--

speaker: interviewee
text: That was in [NON-ENGLISH]. That was not in Krosno.

speaker: interviewer
text: Do you remember your parents' social life? You were a little girl, but do you remember them having parties and-- that were not holidays, Jewish holidays?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yeah. Mainly family. I don't remember the rest. I don't remember. I was very young. I don't remember.

speaker: interviewer
text: And do you remember--

speaker: interviewee
text: I just remember the streets where I went and the schools where I went and the temple and my aunts and uncles. And even this time when I went back, I said, oh, up the hill here, that was my uncle's house. Up the hill this way was my other aunt's business. And just things came back to me.

speaker: interviewer
text: Was-- did you live in a Jewish section of town? Or was it a mixed section?

speaker: interviewee
text: No, not particularly a Jewish section, but there were many, many Jewish families living there.

speaker: interviewer
text: And how did you-- before the war, how did you, the Jewish population, relate as a, as a whole to the Gentile population? Do you remember that?

speaker: interviewee
text: The truth is that the Polish people were always very anti-Semitic. And we, we kept more or less to ourselves because-- when I went back and I saw my neighbor's house, he-- they were Gentiles. And he was a big anti-Semite. And I knew that we were always afraid of him. We stayed away from where he was. And we just-- we children, we never went on the other side where he was. We were just afraid. We just knew that he just, he just hated us for no reason at all.

speaker: interviewer
text: Now, we'll go to Slovakia now. And you had the life there with you staying with family. Were you staying in your own house there?

speaker: interviewee
text: No, we had our own-- yeah, we had our own apartment.

speaker: interviewer
text: You had your own apartment?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yeah. Yes.

speaker: interviewer
text: Do you remember that place?

speaker: interviewee
text: Oh, yes, I remember that place. Yes.

speaker: interviewer
text: Did you share a room with somebody or was it--

speaker: interviewee
text: Did I share the room with somebody? No, I don't think so. My mother was sickly already at that time. So things were not so good. But my family was very good to us. And, [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] and I had a wonderful great-great-uncle. His name was Rudolph Lowy. And he was the [NON-ENGLISH] from the city. That means he was like [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] the main Jewish person in the city. And things got very bad again. They started, first, taking the young women. First, they wanted-- they started taking the young women. Nobody knew where they are taking them. They told them that they are taking them to working camps.

speaker: interviewer
text: This is the young Jewish women?

speaker: interviewee
text: The young Jewish women. But he was in a position where he found out that that's not so. They are not taking them to work. They are taking them to extermination camps.

speaker: interviewer
text: So he managed to make a quarantine for the young women. He said that-- he set up like a, like a hospital and put these young, young Jewish women there. And he said that they have Typhoid fever. And no SS guard or Hlinka guard, which were the Slovakian guards, dared to go in there to get these women. So that sort of postponed a little bit, you know, them getting away-- getting them to camp. But after a while, they found out that this is only a ruse, you know, to, you know-- and they took all these girls away. And I don't know how many survived. And they also took my uncle away. And he never came back. Was this uncle your, your--

speaker: interviewee
text: My mother's uncle. He's my grand uncle. His name was [NON-ENGLISH] Lowy.

speaker: interviewer
text: Mm-hm. And so now you are there with your mother and father and two--

speaker: interviewee
text: And my, and my two brothers, yeah. My oldest brother and my youngest brother. And the one, one remained in Poland, Nathan, the one.

speaker: interviewer
text: Was there a ghetto, or, or were you free to move around?

speaker: interviewee
text: No, we were free. In between razias, we were free, yeah. And when the Russians were moving their front, the Slovaks decided that everybody has to move from eastern Slovakia.

speaker: interviewer
text: When would this have been? What year approximately?

speaker: interviewee
text: I believe that that must have been already 1943-- two or three. I think maybe 1943.

speaker: interviewer
text: So we had the-- so we-- everybody had to flee from there. So we went to a place called Nove Mesto nad Vahom. That was in Slovakia. And there, we took an apartment. We had an apartment. We lived together.

speaker: interviewee
text: My mother died already in Bardejov. We buried her in Bardejov. She did not come with us anymore to Nove Mesto nad Vahom. When we came to Nove Mesto nad Vahom, [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] we rented an apartment, and we stayed there. And in between, we went into hidings.

speaker: interviewer
text: In between-- how do you mean that?

speaker: interviewee
text: Well, also they said, you know, we heard that there's going to be a razia tonight or tomorrow. And we had a few hiding places.

speaker: interviewer
text: This is in the Nove Mesto?

speaker: interviewee
text: Right. My father managed to pay off some people to get us some hiding places. One hiding place I remember was by a family, where they had a hole in the ground in the kitchen where they kept their winter supplies-- their potatoes and things like that. But they cleaned it out, and they put us in there. And I remember that during the night, we wanted to burn a candle or something. And it just won't go on because there was no air there. There was no oxygen.

speaker: interviewer
text: How many people were in that hiding place?

speaker: interviewee
text: We were there-- four people. Just my father. Yes. My father and my two brothers, yeah. And at night, the lady would come. She would bring us something to eat, and she would take out the dirty pail.

speaker: interviewer
text: How long were you there?

speaker: interviewee
text: Oh, for days and days on end.

speaker: interviewer
text: Without coming out at all?

speaker: interviewee
text: Without coming out at all, you know. Maybe she would open up the-- she would open up the, the, the door and, you know, bring the food in, and you know-- and that didn't, that didn't last too long, either. One day they came in, and they caught us.

speaker: interviewer
text: In the hiding place?

speaker: interviewee
text: No. In the apartment. They took my father away. And they took him with my uncle. His name was Mendel Spira. He was my mother's brother. They didn't take us all together. They didn't want the families to be together. So they, so they separated everybody. So they took him away separately. They took away-- they took me away with my brother.

speaker: interviewer
text: Which brother?

speaker: interviewee
text: The younger brother.

speaker: interviewer
text: Say his name. I forgot.

speaker: interviewee
text: Nathan.

speaker: interviewer
text: Nathan. OK.

speaker: interviewee
text: Yeah.

speaker: interviewer
text: Now wait, that was Nathan or Mordechai? Mordechai was the one who was--

speaker: interviewee
text: Mordechai was remained in Poland.

speaker: interviewer
text: And he's the one who was with your grandmother, and you never--

speaker: interviewee
text: Right.

speaker: interviewer
text: Right. OK.

speaker: interviewee
text: So it was Nathan. He was a year and a half younger than me. They took me and him away separately. Then my older brother, Jack, and a cousin, Benjamin, they hid under the beds. When they heard the, the screaming and yelling on the door, they hid under the beds. Well, they didn't even think looking under the bed, because they figured the whole family is in the room, you know. They got everybody. So they left them behind, you know. And they put a seal on the door. And they remained there for two days. After two days, they had to get out. They figured [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] either they'll come and open up the seal and open up the front apartment and find them anyway, and besides, you can't stay there in the apartment, you know. You, you, you have to go and do something, you know, to get away from there.

speaker: interviewer
text: So they got out of there. Of course, they were caught, also. And they were-- we were all taken to Sered, which was, [PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] which was-- what was it? Like a-- [NON-ENGLISH] they called it-- which is like a place where they brought everybody together and from which the trains went. Like a transit station?

speaker: interviewee
text: Right.

speaker: interviewer
text: Kind of a camp.

speaker: interviewee
text: We were all separated. My father was in one, one place. My-- I was with my brother in another place.

speaker: interviewer
text: Were these in barracks?

speaker: interviewee
text: All in Sered, but not in the same bunks. They separated us, though.

speaker: interviewer
text: So you were all in different barracks?

speaker: interviewee
text: All in different barracks in the same place.

speaker: interviewer
text: So this was a special camp that had been built for this purpose?

speaker: interviewee
text: Right. Right. That was just where they processed everybody into the trains. And while we-- oh, I still want to tell the story about Sered when we were in Nove Mesto nad Vahom. Yeah. They, they took us into-- they put us into a place which was-- I believe it must have been like a retirement home or a-- you know, for elderly people. And there they put everybody there before they took us to Sered.

speaker: interviewer
text: Like a ghetto? They made a ghetto?

speaker: interviewee
text: Right. And I had a-- I was there with my little brother. And I had an aunt there. And she has, she had two daughters. And I found out that she said that during the night, there is one piece in the barbed wire there that is cut. And she's going to try to escape with her two daughters. And I begged her. I said, please, [NON-ENGLISH], don't leave us here. Please take us with you. And she promised, children, don't worry. I wouldn't leave you behind. I'm going to take you. And the next morning we got up and she was gone with the children. And I was just so desperate. And I-- to this day, I feel that if she would have taken us along, maybe my brother would be alive because she and her two daughters survived.

speaker: interviewer
text: They did, too?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yeah. Anyway, when we got to Sered--

speaker: interviewer
text: Excuse me, did she ever say why she didn't take you?

speaker: interviewee
text: No, she never said anything. But in a way, you cannot entirely blame her, because she wanted to save herself and her children. And she felt an extra two children would be a burden, you know. They would catch her.

speaker: interviewer
text: Now, in, in Nove Mesto, you, you had been in an apartment. And sometimes you had to go in hiding, but the rest of the time you were in the apartment. But then when they rounded you up and they took your father and, and you and your brother, did they put you in a-- this was like a ghetto, this area. This area was a camp and it had barbed wire. And you were not free to come and go.

speaker: interviewee
text: No.

speaker: interviewer
text: How long were you in there?

speaker: interviewee
text: Oh, I would say maybe four, five days. Yeah.

speaker: interviewer
text: And did they, did they provide food for the people in there?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes, there they provided for food, you know. There was a, there was a kitchen. They provided some food, yeah.

speaker: interviewer
text: And who administered this?

speaker: interviewee
text: The Jewish, the Jewish people.

speaker: interviewer
text: But I mean, who were the guards? Was it, was it Slovakians?

speaker: interviewee
text: Mainly, mainly Slovakian. The Hlinka guarda. Yeah. Mainly. Yeah.

speaker: interviewer
text: So from there, you were taken to Sered.

speaker: interviewee
text: To Sered.

speaker: interviewer
text: How did they take you?

speaker: interviewee
text: By trains.

speaker: interviewer
text: Do you remember the train ride?

speaker: interviewee
text: By trains. Yes, I do remember. We came to Sered. And we came there, and they put us in separate barracks.

speaker: interviewer
text: No, first talk a little bit about the train ride. Were you-- was it like the cattle cars or was it--

speaker: interviewee
text: It was, it was like the cattle cars.

speaker: interviewer
text: Not passenger trains?

speaker: interviewee
text: No, no. It was like the cattle cars.

speaker: interviewer
text: Do you remember how many-- was there lots and lots of people on the trains?

speaker: interviewee
text: There were lots of people. You were a couple hundred people. There were a couple hundred people.

speaker: interviewer
text: All crowded in.

speaker: interviewee
text: All crowded in.

speaker: interviewer
text: And at that point, were you-- who were you with?

speaker: interviewee
text: And everybody took a suitcase along. Everybody thought that, that we will, you know, we will still live, we will need these things, you know. So, you know, but they took-- young, old, children-- everything, you know. People took just their things, their belongings along-- whatever they could-- in a suitcase.

speaker: interviewer
text: Do you remember what you took?

speaker: interviewee
text: I don't remember.

speaker: interviewer
text: Toys or anything?

speaker: interviewee
text: No, I don't remember.

speaker: interviewer
text: OK. Hold on. One second. We're going to change tape.

speaker: interviewee
text: OK. Can I get a tissue?
